coredata.dc:title: Phenanthroline bridging graphitic carbon nitride framework and Fe (II) ions to promote transfer of photogenerated electrons for selective photocatalytic reduction of Nitrophenols
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ying
Liu, Yi
Xu, Yingying
He, Qingshen
Yin, Rongyang
Sun, Pengfei
Dong, Xiaoping
coredata.dc:description: Nitrophenols (NPs) are widely used in industries and highly toxic to ecological environment and human health. Because aminophenols (APs) are important chemicals, catalytic reduction of NPs via efficient and environment-friendly strategies is of great importance. Herein, we developed a green photocatalysis route to efficiently convert NPs to APs using a Fe (II) modified graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) photocatalyst, where phenanthroline units were employed to bridge Fe (II) and carbon nitride framework. The optimized sample P-CN-8-Fe presented significantly improved absorption of visible light, separation of photogenerated charges and carrier transportation in comparison with the pristine g-C3N4 and the modified samples of CN-Fe and P-CN-8. Accordingly, the P-CN-8-Fe showed a high conversion (97%) of p-nitrophenol (p-NP) to p-aminophenol (p-AP) under 2 h visible light irradiation, and meanwhile possessed high photocatalytic durability. Its high activity was also demonstrated through photocatalytic reduction of other NPs and nitrobenzene compounds. Finally, a possible mechanism was proposed for the photocatalytic reduction of p-NP by P-CN-8-Fe. This work provides an effective approach to prepare Fe (II) modified g-C3N4 through the bridging effect of phenanthroline group, which is a potential visible light driven photocatalyst for reduction of nitrobenzene derivatives.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrophenols
Graphitic carbon nitride
Phenanthroline
Fe (II)
Selective reduction

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of phenanthroline bridging Fe (II) modified g-C3N4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of CN (a), CN-Fe (c), P-CN-8 (e) and P-CN-8-Fe (g); TEM images of CN (b), CN-Fe (d), P-CN-8 (f) and P-CN-8-Fe (h); EDS element mapping (j) in the SEM image (i) of P-CN-8-Fe.
XRD patterns (a) and FTIR spectra (b) of different samples.
UV–vis DRS (a) and PL spectra (b) of different samples.
. XPS spectra of CN and P-CN-8-Fe: survey of P-CN-8-Fe (a); Fe 2p spectrum of P-CN-8-Fe (b); C 1 s spectra (c) and N 1 s spectra (d) of CN and P-CN-8-Fe.

UV–vis absorption spectra of p-NP solution before and after adding N2H4 (a); UV–vis absorption spectra of p-NP solution reduced by P-CN-8-Fe (b); effect of N2H4 dosage on reduction of p-NP (c); the reduction curve of p-NP under various experimental conditions (d). The experimental data in Fig. 5 c, d are based on three repeated experiments.

Photocatalytic reduction of p-NP over different photocatalysts under visible light irradiation (a); the corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetic curves (b) and the apparent rate constants (c); cycle stability test of P-CN-8-Fe (d); XRD patterns (e) and FTIR spectra (f) of the fresh P-CN-8-Fe and the recycled P-CN-8-Fe. The experimental data in Fig. 6 a, b, c, d are based on three repeated experiments.
Transient photocurrent responses (a) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots (b) of samples.
Mott-Schott curves (a and b), Tauc plots (c and d), XPS VB spectra (e and f), and band structure (g) of CN and P-CN-8-Fe.
The schematic diagram of P-CN-X-Fe synthesis.
Schematic diagram of p-NP reduction by the P-CN-8-Fe under visible light irradiation.
HPLC determined composition of the reaction solutions before and after the P-CN-8-Fe photocatalysis a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The experimental data in Table 1 are based on three repeated experiments.

head.ce:title: Phenanthroline bridging graphitic carbon nitride framework and Fe (II) ions to promote transfer of photogenerated electrons for selective photocatalytic reduction of Nitrophenols

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Surface & Interface Science of Polymer Materials of Zhejiang Province, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Surface & Interface Science of Polymer Materials of Zhejiang Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Surface & Interface Science of Polymer Materials of Zhejiang Province, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrophenols (NPs) are widely used in industries and highly toxic to ecological environment and human health. Because aminophenols (APs) are important chemicals, catalytic reduction of NPs via efficient and environment-friendly strategies is of great importance. Herein, we developed a green photocatalysis route to efficiently convert NPs to APs using a Fe (II) modified graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) photocatalyst, where phenanthroline units were employed to bridge Fe (II) and carbon nitride framework. The optimized sample P-CN-8-Fe presented significantly improved absorption of visible light, separation of photogenerated charges and carrier transportation in comparison with the pristine g-C3N4 and the modified samples of CN-Fe and P-CN-8. Accordingly, the P-CN-8-Fe showed a high conversion (97%) of p-nitrophenol (p-NP) to p-aminophenol (p-AP) under 2 h visible light irradiation, and meanwhile possessed high photocatalytic durability. Its high activity was also demonstrated through photocatalytic reduction of other NPs and nitrobenzene compounds. Finally, a possible mechanism was proposed for the photocatalytic reduction of p-NP by P-CN-8-Fe. This work provides an effective approach to prepare Fe (II) modified g-C3N4 through the bridging effect of phenanthroline group, which is a potential visible light driven photocatalyst for reduction of nitrobenzene derivatives.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nitrophenol (NP) compounds in wastewater have attracted more attention because of their high toxicity, nonbiodegradability, and high discharge from industrial sources, such as plastics, petrochemicals, dyes, pesticides, medicine, textiles and steel production [1,2]. The methods for removing NPs mainly include physical, biological and chemical methods. Among them, physical methods achieve the purpose of eliminating NPs by enriching them, but the disposal of adsorbents is more difficult. The microbial method can degrade NPs to a certain extent; however, the toxic effect of partial intermediate products limits the effective operation of this method [3,4]. Chemical methods, including oxidation and reduction, are considered to be feasible methods to remove NPs. Advanced oxidation processes, e.g., the Fenton method, photo-promoted Fenton route and O3 treatment, have been demonstrated as efficient treatment strategies that can directly decompose NPs to mineralized small molecules [5–8]. These approaches are not ideal for product selectivity and sometimes even lead to the formation of a variety of low-carbon byproducts, resulting in secondary environmental pollution.

Aminophenol (AP) compounds are reduction products of NPs, which are widely used in the pharmaceutical industry as intermediates, and can also be used as antioxidants and petroleum additives [9,10]. Therefore, selective conversion of NPs to APs not only can eliminate related environmental pollution problems but also has an important economic purpose. In fact, there have been related technologies to reduce NPs to APs, including iron powder reduction and catalytic hydrogenation [11–13]. However, the iron powder reduction is very inefficient and will produce a large amount of iron sludge. Catalytic hydrogenation occurs in an acid or alkali medium under the action of a catalyst, using molecular hydrogen or NaBH4 as hydrogen sources. Despite the high product quality, the catalytic hydrogenation requires highly specialized reaction equipment, and the catalyst is hard to recycle [14]. Compared with these methods, visible-light-driven (VLD) photocatalytic reduction displays the advantages of economic benefits, environmental friendliness, and low energy consumption [15,16]. Therefore, it will be of great scientific significance to develop a photocatalytic method for selectively converting NPs into APs.

As a VLD nonmetal semiconductor with a band gap of ∼ 2.7 eV, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has a wide range of applications in photocatalysis, including the production of hydrogen by splitting water, restoring CO2 and removing different kinds of environmental pollutants [17–20]. Although g-C3N4 has good thermal stability and chemical stability, there are also shortcomings, such as serious recombination of photogenerated carriers, low visible light response and small specific surface area, resulting in unsatisfactory photocatalytic performance [21–23]. A series of methods have been developed to improve the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4, including construction of heterojunctions with other semiconductors, element doping, morphology control, and covalent modification of small organic molecules [19,24–26]. Furthermore, the metal complex can control the light absorption, catalytic active sites and charge dynamics of g-C3N4 [27]. As the fourth element in crustal elements, iron is environmentally friendly and biofriendly. It can participate in the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, as Fe (II) possesses reducing properties and Fe (III) displays oxidizing properties [28]. Yao et al. achieved highly dispersed and stable Fe (III) active sites on g-C3N4 by the chelating effect of modified ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), of which the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation rate was 13.5 times of that of g-C3N4 [29]. The enhanced separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs was ascribed to the changed π conjugate network resulting from the electron withdrawing effect of the Fe-EDTA unit. Wu et al. coordinated iron hexadecachlorophthalocyanine (FePcCl16) with g-C3N4 and the prepared photocatalyst showed improved performance for removing carbamazepine by activating peroxymonosulfate, which was attributed to the promoted electron migration from FePcCl16 to g-C3N4 [30]. The phenanthroline with a tricyclic conjugated rigid structure is a reagent for testing trace iron in analytical chemistry. The conjugated aromatic structure is benefit for enhancing the electron cloud density of the N atom, and therefore strengthens the d-π bond with the empty orbital of the metal ion. Furthermore, it has been proved that phenanthroline is a promising electron acceptor to trap and stabilize the received electron due to good planarity and a high degree of conjugation [31]. Khnayzer et al. reported that phenanthroline-Cu (I) complexes could serve as visible light photocatalysts and presented efficient activity for homogeneous catalytic hydrogen evolution [32].

In this work, we fabricated a new Fe-modified g-C3N4 composite catalyst using phenanthroline unit as a bridge to connect the framework of g-C3N4 and Fe (II), and studied its selectively catalytic reduction of NPs to APs. The obtained phenanthroline bridging Fe-modified g-C3N4 photocatalysts showed enhanced visible light absorption, separation rate of photogenerated charges and VLD photocatalytic activity for reduction of NPs to APs. We also investigated the photocatalytic universality for reduction of different NPs and nitrobenzene compounds, stability and catalytic durability of photocatalysts, and finally proposed a possible photocatalytic mechanism based on the determined energy band structure.

Melamine (>99 %) was purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd., phenanthroline (AR) was provided by Tianjin Kemeou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., FeSO4·7H2O (AR), FeCl3·6H2O (AR), glacial acetic acid (HAc, AR), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, AR) and hydrazine hydrate (N2H4, AR) were obtained from Hangzhou Gaojing Chemical Co., Ltd., anhydrous sodium acetate (NaAc, AR) was supplied by Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., p-nitrophenol (p-NP, >99.5%), o-nitrophenol (o-NP, AR), m-nitrophenol (m-NP, >99%), p-chloronitrobenzene (>99%), p-bromonitrobenzene (>99%), p-nitrobenzoic acid (98%) were provided by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., and nitrobenzene (99%) and p-aminophenol (p-AP, >98%) were supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Deionized water was purified by MILL-Q system of Millipore of which the resistivity > 18.2 M Ω cm−1.

The pristine g-C3N4 (denoted as CN) sample was prepared according to our previous works [33,34], and the synthesis of phenanthroline modified g-C3N4 employed a similar procedure. Typically, 0.16 g phenanthroline and 4 g melamine were mixed in an agate mortar and then the mixture was transferred into an alumina crucible with a cover. After heating it at 550 °C for 4 h in a muffle furnace, the resultant brown solid was ground to powder, marked as P-CN-8, the “8″ represents the phenanthroline/g-C3N4 mass ratio in percentage (the yield of g-C3N4 using melamine as precursor is ∼ 50%). Similarly, we prepared samples of P-CN-0.5, P-CN-1, P-CN-2, P-CN-4 and P-CN-16.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phenanthroline bridging Fe (II) modified g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared P-CN-X (X = 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8 and 16) sample was added into a FeSO4 solution with a 1:1 M ratio of phenanthroline/FeSO4, the pH of which was adjusted to 5 using a HAc-NaAc buffer solution. After stirring the suspension at room temperature for 4 h, the P-CN-8-Fe was centrifuged, washed with deionized water, and finally dried at 80 °C overnight. For comparison, a Fe (II) modified g-C3N4 without phenanthroline bridging and Fe (III) modified P-CN-8 were prepared under the same conditions, marked as CN-Fe and P-CN-8-Fe (III). The synthesis diagram of the catalysts is shown in the Scheme 1 .

The morphology and element distribution of the samples were obtained by a Hitachi SU-8100 field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation was performed on a JEOL-2100 electron microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The lattice structures of the materials were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, DX-2700). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on a Nicolet Avatar 370 spectrophotometer. With BaSO4 as a reference, UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were measured on a Shimadzu 2450 UV–Vis spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra were measured using an Edinburgh FL/FS900 spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using multifunctional electron spectroscopy (Kratos, AXIS, Ultra, DLD). The electrochemical properties were studied on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Chenhua, Shanghai, China).

We firstly evaluated the VLD photocatalytic activity for reduction of NPs via using p-NP as a model pollutant. Typically, 50 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed into 50 mL p-NP (5 mg/L) solution containing 0.02 M N2H4 under stirring. Nitrogen was injected into the suspension to remove dissolved oxygen, resulting in a nitrogen atmosphere in the reactor before stirring in the dark for 1 h to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The photocatalytic reaction of p-NP was realized under visible light irradiation with a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 420 nm cutoff filter. Then, 3.5 mL of the reaction suspension was removed every 30 min, centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 5 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove solid materials. The filtered solution was analyzed by a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimazu UV-2450) to detect the absorbance change of the reaction solution at λ = 400 nm. All the experimental data are the average values of three repeated experiments.

p-NP was further analyzed by a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system equipped with a WondaSil C18 (4.6 × 200 mm, 5 μm). The buffer solution was 10 mM KH2PO4, the pH of which was adjusted to 2.5 with H3PO4, the volumetric ratio of CH3OH and KH2PO4 was 9:20, and the flow rate was 1.0 mL/min.

Other NPs, e.g. o-NP and m-NP, and other nitrobenzene compounds, e.g. nitrobenzene, p-chloronitrobenzene, p-bromonitrobenzene, and p-nitrobenzoic acid, were also used to evaluate the photocatalytic reduction performance of the obtained catalysts. The reaction procedures were similar to that of p-NP.

The electrochemical test was carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) with 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte solution. Typically, the three-electrode system consisted of Pt as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and indium tin oxide (ITO) conductive glass coated with materials as the working electrode. The preparation of the working electrode was as follows: 25 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 1 mL of 5 wt% polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) solution and stirred for 3 h. The obtained mixture was coated on a 3 cm × 1 cm ITO conductive glass by spin coating, which was then dried overnight at 80 °C.

The copolymerization pathway of melamine and phenanthroline is shown in Fig. S1 . At high temperature, melem free radicals and ·NH2 radicals would be generated during the polymerization of melamine. Similar to the free radical mechanism for biphenyl synthesis from aniline ( Fig. S2 ), the phenanthroline unit could be connected to the CN skeleton via a free radical process. Considering the steric hindrance effect, melem free radicals attack the ortho and para CH of the N atom in phenanthroline. The removed H· would react with ·NH2 to release NH3, and the CC covalent bonds between C atom in terminal C-NH2 of the CN skeleton and CH of phenanthroline would form.

The photocatalytic reduction of p-NP over the P-CN-X-Fe is shown in Fig. S3 . Compared to the pristine CN, all P-CN-X-Fe catalysts display enhanced photocatalytic performance, and among them the P-CN-8-Fe possesses the best photocatalytic activity. The surface morphologies of CN (Fig. 1
a), CN-Fe (Fig. 1 c), P-CN-8 (Fig. 1 e) and P-CN-8-Fe (Fig. 1 g) are shown in SEM images, all of which exhibit irregularly stacked block structures and rough surfaces. The morphologies of CN (Fig. 1 b), CN-Fe (Fig. 1 d), P-CN-8 (Fig. 1 f) and P-CN-8-Fe (Fig. 1 h) are further examined by TEM observation, which exhibit large-scale amorphous layered structures. The EDS elemental mapping of P-CN-8-Fe in Fig. 1 j demonstrates that C, N and Fe are evenly distributed, which verifies the successful fabrication of the phenanthroline chelated Fe (II) complex on the g-C3N4 framework.

XRD patterns of CN, CN-Fe, P-CN-8 and P-CN-8-Fe are depicted in Fig. 2
a. All samples exhibit two diffraction peaks at 13.1° and 27.4°, which are indexed to the (100) and (002) planes of the pristine CN [35]. In addition, the (100) facet corresponds to the regular in-plane arrangement of the heptazine unit, while the (002) diffraction represents the order stacking of g-C3N4 layers [36]. These results indicate that all the modified samples still remain the graphite-like structure after incorporation of phenanthroline and Fe (II). However, the diffraction intensities significantly decrease, which is ascribed to the disordered distribution of phenanthroline units and Fe (II) ions. Apparently, the content of grafted phenanthroline unit in skeleton increases, the structural ordering decreases. It can be indicated in Fig. S4 , where the diffraction intensity gradually declines as increasing the phenanthroline content. Furthermore, no diffraction peak of Fe species is found, suggesting that the Fe (II) ions are highly dispersed and chelated by functional groups on the P-CN-8 skeleton.

Fig. 2 b shows the characteristic peaks of the modified CN photocatalysts in the FTIR spectra. Two broad absorption bands at ∼ 3200 and ∼ 3500 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration mode of NH belonging to the terminal amino group of CN [37] and the OH stretching vibration of water [38]. It is obviously found that the P-CN-8 and the P-CN-8-Fe sample have a bigger relative intensity of the ∼ 3500 cm−1 absorption than the CN sample, which indicates the successful incorporation of phenanthroline into the g-C3N4 framework. The vibration peaks at 1200–1600 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of sp3 CN and sp2 CN [39]. The sharp peak at 810 cm−1 originates from the typical stretching vibration of the heptazine unit [40]. These characteristic absorptions can be observed in all the modified samples, implying that the g-C3N4 structure is preserved well after the addition of phenanthroline and Fe (II). Additionally, no obvious peaks of phenanthroline are found, which should be ascribed to its low concentration in the framework.

UV–vis DRS was employed to characterize the optical properties of samples. As displayed in Fig. 3
a, the color of pure CN and CN-Fe is yellow, while after introducing phenanthroline the samples became dark brown. It can be further demonstrated by the DRS results. The absorption spectrum of CN exhibits an absorption edge at ∼ 460 nm, and the CN-Fe gives a similar optical absorption behavior. However, besides the band-to-band transition, strong absorption in the whole visible region is also observed in the P-CN-8 and P-CN-8-Fe samples, which should originate from the introduction of phenanthroline and the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) in the phenanthroline-Fe (II) complex. The existence of the LMCT phenomenon indicates that the phenanthroline ferrous complex has an electron-withdrawing effect, which can effectively prevent the recombination of photogenerated carriers and improve the photocatalytic performance.

For photocatalyst, besides the light absorption to generated electron-hole pairs, its activity is also strongly dependent on the effective separation of these photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Fig. 3 b compares the PL spectra of CN, CN-Fe, P-CN-8 and P-CN-8-Fe samples. The addition of phenanthroline or Fe (II) can significantly decrease the PL intensity, implying that the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs is efficiently suppressed. Among these photocatalysts, the P-CN-8-Fe sample possesses the lowest PL intensity, indicating it maybe has the best photocatalytic performance [41]. TRPL spectra of the samples are shown in Fig. S5 . The fluorescence lifetimes are 3.45 ns for CN, 3.39 ns for CN-Fe, 2.30 ns for P-CN-8, and 2.19 ns for P-CN-8-Fe, respectively. The decrease in the average lifetime indicates that the enhanced dissociation of singlet exciton in the photocatalyst and charge transfer kinetics [42]. The TRPL results imply that the introduction of phenanthroline-Fe (II) unit promoted the transfer kinetics of photogenerated charges.

XPS measurements were used to study the surface element compositions and their chemical environments. Fig. 4
a depicts the XPS survey of P-CN-8-Fe, where four elements, C, N, O, and Fe, were checked. The Fe 2p spectrum of P-CN-8-Fe (see Fig. 4 b) presents four characteristic peaks of Fe (II) species, where the 709.5 eV signal and its satellite peak (714.3 eV) originate from Fe 2p3/2, and the 722.6 eV absorption and its satellite peak (729.7 eV) are related to Fe 2p1/2 [43]. For comparison, the Fe 2p spectrum of Fe (III) modified P-CN-8 is shown in Fig. S6 . The Fe 2p3/2 peaks of Fe (III) are located at 711.4 eV and 717.2 eV, and its Fe 2p1/2 signals appear at 725.0 eV and 732.9 eV [43]. The above results indicate that the iron in P-CN-8-Fe exists as Fe (II) instead of Fe (III). The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4 c) and the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4 d) of the P-CN-8-Fe are similar to those of the CN sample. The C 1 s spectra were deconvoluted into two peaks at approximately 287.7 eV and 284.6 eV. The 287.7 eV signal is attributed to the carbon atom in the NC-N group of g-C3N4, and the peak at 284.6 eV originates from the CC/CC units [44]. Compared to the CN, the P-CN-8-Fe has a large relative peak area for the 284.6 eV band, which can be ascribed to the phenanthroline doping. The N 1 s peaks were divided into three peaks: the peak at 398.8 eV is assigned to the sp2 hybrid nitrogen atom in the CN heterocycle (CN-C), the peak at 399.7 eV corresponds to the tertiary nitrogen atom connecting the carbon atom bond in N-(C)3, and the peak at 400.9 eV results from the NH of the terminal amino group [45]. In addition, the relative peak area of the CN-C group in the P-CN-8-Fe sample is smaller than that in the CN. This change is also contributed by the phenanthroline doping, which is consistent with the result in the C 1 s spectra.

The photocatalytic reduction performance of the prepared P-CN-8-Fe photocatalyst was firstly studied via reduction of p-NP to p-AP under visible light irradiation. Fig. 5
a shows that the absorption band of p-NP is centered at 316 nm, and it shifted to 400 nm as adding N2H4, which is ascribed to the p-NP anion from the acid-base reaction between p-NP and N2H4 [46]. Fig. 5 b presents the change in absorption spectrum of reaction solution. The absorption at 400 nm gradually decreased as prolonging the irradiation time, and meanwhile a new absorption appeared at 297 nm that is determined to p-AP. It is consistent with the absorption band of the p-AP standard solution. Moreover, two isosbestic points at 276 and 310 nm indicate that p-AP is the only product and no byproducts were formed in the photocatalytic reduction reaction [47,48]. HPLC measurements ( Fig. S7 ) also prove this conclusion. The retention times of p-NP and p-AP are determined to be 8.00 and 2.67 min, respectively, from the HPLC spectra of their standard solutions. After the photocatalytic reaction, the p-NP peak intensity was greatly decreased, and an intensive p-AP signal appeared. No other peak was found, proving that p-NP has been highly selectively reduced to p-AP.

The N2H4 concentration was optimized in Fig. 5 c through studying the photocatalytic performance using three N2H4 concentrations (0.01 M, 0.02 M and 0.04 M). In the reduction process of nitro group to amino group, N2H4 acts as an electron and proton donor. A low N2H4 concentration is insufficient to quench the photoexcited hole on the valance band (VB) of photocatalyst, while overmany N2H4 molecules will occupy the photocatalyst surface to suppress the electron transfer from the conduction band (CB) of photocatalyst to the p-NP. As a result, the best photocatalytic activity was achieved using a medium N2H4 concentration of 0.02 M. Fig. 5 d shows the photocatalytic behavior of p-NP reduction under different reaction conditions. As one of the experimental conditions, photocatalyst, light irradiation and N2H4, was absent, there was no apparent decrease of p-NP concentration and meanwhile no p-AP was generated ( Fig. S8 and Table S1). The photocatalytic process of converting p-NP to p-AP can be efficiently driven only in the co-presence of photocatalyst, light irradiation and N2H4.

The VLD photocatalytic behavior of the P-CN-8-Fe photocatalyst for reduction of nitrobenzene derivatives was also studied and the results are listed in Table 1 . Under the identical conditions, the nitrobenzene conversion efficiency (Entry 1, 90%) is much lower than those of NPs (Entry 2, 3, and 4), in spite of the electron-donating property of the hydroxyl group on benzene ring that is against the nitro reduction [49]. It should be attributed to the ionization of NPs in the alkaline solution, where the NP anions are electrostatically attracted by the chelated Fe (II) cation on the photocatalyst and facilely accepts photogenerated electron from the Fe (II) active site. As electron-withdrawing substituted groups (-Cl, -Br and –COOH) were introduced, the conversion ratios of these nitrobenzene derivatives (Entry 5, 6 and 7) are also higher than that of the nitrobenzene, which originates from the decrease of electron cloud density on benzene ring that is benefit to the nitro reduction. The above results suggest that the P-CN-8-Fe photocatalyst can effectively photoreduce a variety of nitrobenzene compounds.

Fig. 6
a compares the photocatalytic performance of P-CN-8-Fe with the pristine CN, CN-Fe and P-CN-8 samples. The p-NP conversion efficiency on the P-CN-8-Fe is 97% in a 2 h photocatalytic reaction, which is much higher than those of CN (6%), CN-Fe (38%) and P-CN-8 (27%). A pseudo-first-order kinetic formula (lnC/C 0 = -k • t) was employed to study the photocatalytic kinetics [50]. The well linearity of the photocatalytic data (Fig. 6 b) implies that the photocatalytic process follows a pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics, and the apparent rate constant (k) of P-CN-8-Fe (Fig. 6 c) is calculated to be 1.46 h−1, which is 48 times, 6 times and 9 times of those using CN (0.03 h−1), CN-Fe (0.23 h−1) and P-CN-8 (0.16 h−1) as photocatalysts, respectively.

The photocatalytic durability and stability of the P-CN-8-Fe photocatalyst was also tested, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 d-f. In the successive five photocatalytic cycles, the p-NP conversion ratio is maintained at 95 ∼ 98%, indicating the well photocatalytic durability of the P-CN-8-Fe. There is no evident difference in the XRD patterns and FTIR spectra between the fresh and the recycled P-CN-8-Fe, also demonstrating the high structural stability of this photocatalyst.

The photoinduced carrier separation and migration of the photocatalysts are vital to photocatalytic performance, which were revealed by these electrochemical measurements of transient photocurrent response (Fig. 7
a) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 7 b) [51]. The order of photocurrent intensity is CN < P-CN-8 < CN-Fe < P-CN-8-Fe, suggesting the P-CN-8-Fe has the best separation and transportation capability of photogenerated charge carriers among these photocatalysts. It is worth noting that the photocurrent response of P-CN-8-Fe is stable without obvious attenuation in every turn-on light cycle. The radius of the EIS arc is related to the charge transfer resistance of the photocatalysts, and a smaller radius of the EIS arc corresponds to a smaller resistance [52]. The order of the arc radius from large to small is CN > P-CN-8 > CN-Fe > P-CN-8-Fe, which is consistent with the results obtained from the photocurrent tests. The above results prove that the introduction of the phenanthroline-Fe (II) complex improves the separation efficiency and migration properties of the photoexcited electrons and holes generated on g-C3N4, which is beneficial to electron transfer efficiency in the p-NP reduction reaction.

To further understand the origin of the improved photocatalytic performance, we calculated the band structure of P-CN-8-Fe and CN (see Fig. 8 ). The semiconductor type and flat-band potential (generally regarded as the Fermi level, Ef) were determined by the Mott-Schottky (MS) electrochemical tests. The CN and P-CN-8-Fe are both n-type semiconductors due to their positive slopes, and their Ef values relative to Ag/AgCl are −1.24 V and −1.31 V, converted into reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) potential by the following formula [35,53]:
ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.198 eV + 0.0592 pH

Among them, the pH of the 0.5 M NaSO4 electrolyte solution is 6.8, and therefore we calculated the Ef of CN and P-CN-8-Fe to be −0.64 eV and −0.71 eV vs. RHE, respectively. According to their DRS data, the band gaps of CN and P-CN-8-Fe were calculated to be 2.76 eV and 2.75 eV, respectively. The relative potentials between the VB and the Ef obtained by XPS VB spectroscopy were 2.42 eV for CN and 2.09 eV for P-CN-8-Fe. Based on the above results, the CB value of CN and P-CN-8-Fe were determined to −0.98 eV and −1.37 eV respectively, and the band gap diagram of the materials is shown in Fig. 8 g. By comparison, it is found that the P-CN-8-Fe has a much higher CB edge potential than the pristine CN, which means the photoexcited electrons on the CB of P-CN-8-Fe has much stronger reduction ability. The CB negative shift of P-CN-8-Fe should be related to the incorporation of phenanthroline unit in the CN framework that contributed an improvement of electronic conjugation [54]. Additionally, the raise of VB edge in P-CN-8-Fe is attributed to the introduction of a Fe (II) occupied 3d level [55]. In addition, the gap between the Ef and the CB on the P-CN-8-Fe is larger than that on the CN. It suggests the modification of phenanthroline-Fe (II) complex with electron withdrawing ability can induce the decrease in density of electron cloud on the g-C3N4 framework.

On the basis of the above results, a possible mechanism of the photocatalytic reduction of p-NP by the P-CN-8-Fe was proposed, as shown in Scheme 2
. The photocatalyst absorbs photons to generate photogenerated electron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation. Due to the electron-withdrawing property of the phenanthroline-Fe (II) unit, photoexcited electrons and holes are highly separated. By reacting with photogenerated hole, the N2H4 molecule is oxidized to N2 and H+ [47]. At the same time, the Fe (II) transfers electron to p-NP and then is oxidized to Fe (III), which is further reduced to Fe (II) by photogenerated electron migrating from the g-C3N4 framework. After accepting the electrons from Fe sites and the protons from the oxidation of N2H4, the p-NP is reduced to p-AP.

In summary, we have successfully modified Fe (II) onto the g-C3N4 framework through the bridging effect of phenanthroline unit. Besides the enhanced visible light absorption, the grafted phenanthroline-Fe (II) complex acted as an electron acceptor to improve the separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. The prepared photocatalyst of P-CN-8-Fe could efficiently drive the photocatalytic reduction of NPs to APs. For example, the photocatalytic reaction rate of p-NP to p-AP on the P-CN-8-Fe was 1.46 h−1, which was 47 times, 5 times and 8 times higher than those on the CN, CN-Fe and P-CN-8, respectively. In addition, we compared the P-CN-8-Fe with the photocatalysts in reported literatures for photocatalytic reduction of p-NP (Table S2), and found that the P-CN-8-Fe has a better photocatalytic performance than most of these photocatalysts. The high photocatalytic stability was also proved by successive five runs. Through determining the band structure, it was found that the P-CN-8-Fe had a much higher CB edge potential than that of the pristine CN, indicating the much stronger photoreduction ability of P-CN-8-Fe than the CN. Furthermore, the gap increased between the CB and the Ef of the P-CN-8-Fe compared to the CN, which reflected the decrease of electron cloud density of g-C3N4 framework after modifying the phenanthroline-Fe (II). This work not only demonstrates that the covalent modification of metal complex is an efficient strategy to promote the light absorption and charge separation on the g-C3N4 photocatalyst, but also develops a promising VLD photocatalyst to convert NPs to APs through an environment-friendly route.

Ying Liu: Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Yi Liu: Validation, Visualization. Yingying Xu: Data curation. Qingshen He: Visualization, Methodology. Rongyang Yin: Validation, Methodology. Pengfei Sun: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoping Dong: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFE0127400), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51908491), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LY20B070001), and the Science Foundation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (NO.21062255-Y).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.10.146.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
